era_context: The mid-4th century BCE continues with Philip II of Macedon in the ascendant, Greek city-states fractured and competing, the Persian Achaemenid Empire at its territorial peak, and the Warring States period in China intensifying. This is a transitional era in which the old Greek polis system is being challenged by Macedonian military innovation and political consolidation, while Eastern empires remain dominant in their respective regions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (no contemporary written records)

events.title: Demosthenes delivers the First Philippic against Philip II
events.description: Demosthenes delivers his First Philippic (Olynthiac oration) in the Athenian Assembly, warning of Philip II's ambitions and calling for Athenian military action to counter Macedonian expansion. The speech is a landmark in Greek political rhetoric and represents the ideological opposition of the democratic city-states to Macedonian hegemony. Demosthenes argues that Philip's military campaigns threaten Greek freedom and that Athens must act decisively.

sources.name: Demosthenes, First Philippic (primary text, surviving speech)
Aeschines, Against Demosthenes (primary text, contemporary response)

events.certainty_note: The First Philippic survives as a complete text. Ancient sources (Plutarch, Diodorus) date it to 351 BCE. The speech itself contains internal references to contemporary events that support this dating.

events.title: Philip II campaigns in Thessaly and consolidates influence over northern Greece
events.description: Philip II extends Macedonian influence into Thessaly, a region of strategic importance for controlling access to central Greece. Through a combination of military campaigns and diplomatic alliances, Philip gains the support of Thessalian nobles and begins to position Macedon as the dominant power in northern Greece. This expansion alarms the southern Greek city-states, particularly Athens and Thebes.
events.certainty_note: The sequence of Philip's Thessalian campaigns is attested in later sources. Exact dating is uncertain; scholarly consensus places major Thessalian involvement in the early 350s BCE, with 351 BCE as a conventional assignment.
cross_references: Leads to -348-001 (Philip's intervention in Thessalian affairs)

events.title: Artaxerxes III Ochus rules the Persian Achaemenid Empire at its territorial zenith
events.description: Artaxerxes III continues to rule the Achaemenid Empire, which at this point encompasses territories from Egypt to the Indus Valley. The empire is administratively complex and economically productive, but internal strains are beginning to accumulate. Artaxerxes III's reign represents the last period of strong centralized Persian imperial power before the empire's decline in the late 4th century BCE.
events.certainty_note: Artaxerxes III's reign (358–338 BCE) is well-established. The year 351 BCE falls within his reign with certainty. Specific events for this year are less well-documented.

events.title: Warring States period in China: Qi, Chu, Zhao, and other powers consolidate territory
events.description: During the Warring States period, the major Chinese states—Qi, Chu, Zhao, Wei, and others—continue to consolidate power and expand their territories at the expense of smaller states. This period is characterized by intense military competition, administrative innovation, and the development of new military technologies (crossbows, siege warfare). The era will culminate in the unification of China under the Qin dynasty in 221 BCE.
key_figures: Various rulers of Qi, Chu, Zhao, Wei, and other states (names and specific roles uncertain for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian (1st century BCE, but based on earlier records)
Bamboo Annals (Zhuangzi and other texts, compiled later but containing earlier material)

events.certainty_note: The Warring States period is well-documented in Chinese sources, but specific events for individual years are often uncertain. The dating of events in this period is approximate within ±5–10 years. Chinese chronology for the 4th century BCE has been revised multiple times by modern scholars.
cross_references: Part of broader Warring States period (-475 to -221)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Demosthenes' First Philippic has been debated by scholars. While ancient sources and the surviving text suggest 351 BCE, some modern historians argue for 350 BCE or even 349 BCE. The internal chronological references in the speech are ambiguous and have been interpreted differently. Additionally, the significance of Demosthenes' early Philippics in shaping Athenian policy has been questioned; some scholars argue that Athenian foreign policy was driven more by pragmatic considerations than by Demosthenes' rhetoric. The portrayal of Philip II as an inevitable threat is a retrospective interpretation; in 351 BCE, many Greeks still viewed Macedon as a peripheral power.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 351 BCE is heavily weighted toward Greek sources, particularly Athenian political discourse. Persian sources are mediated through Greek historiographers. Chinese sources are more contemporary but focus on internal state dynamics with no cross-cultural documentation. The survival of Demosthenes' speeches creates an illusion of detailed knowledge about Athenian politics while obscuring the perspectives of other Greek city-states and non-Greek peoples. The bias toward political and military history reflects both source survival and historiographic tradition.

graph_edges.note: Demosthenes' First Philippic called for Athenian support for Olynthus, which was besieged by Philip in 349 BCE
Philip's Thessalian consolidation enabled his later intervention in central Greek affairs
Artaxerxes III's rule set the stage for the succession crisis and the eventual fall of the Achaemenid Empire
